days.headline: A lumpy transition through the foothills of the Chartreuse to Voiron — nominally a sprint stage, but textured enough that a well-drilled breakaway could steal it before the high Alps arrive in earnest.

picks.why: Versatile enough to win from a reduced group if the terrain shells the pure sprinters before the line.
Has been a fixture on the podium in reduced bunch finishes all race.
A craft breakaway rider who specialises in exactly this kind of lumpy, last-chance-before-the-mountains stage.

days.headline: A long Alpine haul to the historic ski station at Orcières-Merlette — a steady rather than savage finale that suits a sustained, team-led GC assault.

picks.why: The strongest team and the strongest rider on any climb where a long, sustained effort rather than a single sharp kick decides things.
A strong, consistent climber who has stayed within touching distance of the GC lead all race and has the legs for a long, steady grind like this.
Has climbed consistently with the GC group all race and a long, even gradient plays to his engine.

days.headline: Short, sharp and explosive: under 130 km from Gap to the 21 hairpins of Alpe d'Huez — a stage built for chaos and long-range attacks rather than a controlled train.

picks.why: The rider best equipped to go long-range on a short mountain stage where explosive, decisive racing suits him most.
Has stayed glued to the front of the GC group in the mountains all race and is a threat to move on a stage built for chaos.
An aggressive climber who thrives in the kind of unpredictable, attack-heavy racing a short summit finish like this breeds.

days.headline: The queen stage and the last mountain day of the race — over 5,600 m of climbing finishing up Alpe d'Huez a second day running, the final chance to make or defend the GC.

picks.why: The dominant climber of the race and the rider with the most to defend on the last mountain stage of the Tour.
A strong, consistent climbing threat who has stayed within touching distance of the GC lead all race — the last mountain day is a final chance to press that attack.
The revelation of this Tour in the high mountains, with the legs and team support to threaten a GC podium finish on the toughest climbing day of the race.

days.headline: The processional ride into Paris — champagne, photos and truce until the race hits the Champs-Élysées circuit, then the most prestigious sprint of the season.

picks.why: Locks in the green jersey and has the pure speed to add a stage win in Paris to cap his Tour.
Has been the most consistent fast finisher in reduced and full bunch sprints all race and will fancy his chances on the Champs-Élysées.
A prolific bunch sprinter who will target the biggest sprint of the season as a fitting finale.

fantasy.strategy: The stage 15-21 transfer window remains open, and the run-in from stage 17 is climbing-heavy: three summit finishes across stages 18-20, including back-to-back trips up Alpe d'Huez, bookended by a lumpy transition day (17) and the ceremonial sprint into Paris (21). Prioritise your strongest GC climbers for that Alpine block, and keep a genuine breakaway punt in your back pocket for stage 17 before the racing gets serious. Save the double-points bonus stage for stage 20 — the longest and hardest climbing day left in the race — and hold a pure sprinter or two in reserve for the points and stage-win value of the Paris finale.
